segments.s647-g129: چو introduces a conditional clause meaning 'if' (classical/poetic usage). پیمان آزادگان is the object phrase 'the pact/pledge of the free' (possessive/genitive relation). بشکنی is a present-subjunctive/optative 2nd-person singular form ('you break' / 'you should break') used after چو to state the protasis ('if you break...').
segments.s647-g130: This is the apodosis (main clause). نشان بزرگی = 'the sign/token of greatness' (نشان modified by بزرگی). به خاک is a prepositional phrase 'into/on the dust/earth', and افگنی is the verbal form (2nd-person) meaning 'you cast/throw' used here as the result clause verb; the verb follows the object and complements in poetic word order.
segments.s647-g131: مرا با تو پیمان = 'the covenant between me and you' (مرا here functions as 'me' + object marker را in the text split); بباید is a modal auxiliary 'must/ought', and شکست is the verbal noun/root meaning 'be broken' or 'breaking'—together بباید شکست expresses necessity ('must be broken'). Word order is topical ('me/with you the covenant') followed by the necessity construction.
segments.s647-g132: به ناچار is an adverbial phrase 'of necessity / inevitably' (به + ناچار). بردن is the infinitive 'to take/draw' (here 'to take up'), and به شمشیر دست is an idiomatic phrase 'hand to the sword' = 'to draw the sword / take up the sword' where دست ('hand') completes the image; the whole segment gives the consequence/action required by the previous clause.
segments.s647-g133: به نامه = 'by letter / in a letter' indicating means. ز هر کارش = 'of every one of his deeds' (ز marks the source/subject-matter, هر کارش = each of his acts). آگاه کرد is a past-tense causative 'he made (someone) aware' = 'he informed (him)'. The verb کرد is simple past 3sg.
segments.s647-g134: بسی is an intensifier 'many/much' (classical usage). هدیه is the direct object 'gifts/offerings'. با نامه همراه کرد = 'he accompanied (it) with the letter' / 'he sent along with the letter' where همراه acts as 'together/along with' and کرد is past tense 'he did/sent'. The verb governs the object and the accompanying phrase.
segments.s647-g135: This is a nominal list: سواری (rider/horseman), سراینده (poet/songster), و (and) سرفراز (proud/victorious one). Word order lists epithets/titles; these nouns jointly serve as the subject for the following verb.
segments.s647-g136: همی‌رفت is an imperfective past/habitual past 'was going / went on his way'. با نامه is a prepositional phrase 'with the letter' indicating accompaniment, and خوشنواز modifies نامه ('melodious' or 'pleasing-sounding') — together: 'he was going with the melodious letter'.
segments.s647-g57: This is a list introduced by the preposition z (ز) = “from,” enumerating places. The sequence uses poetic coordi­nation: ز کوه و بیابان (from mountain and wilderness) then وز (written together here) functions as the equivalent of و + ز = “and from,” continuing the list: دشت و غار (plain and cave). Word order is the Persian normal preposition + noun, with coordinating و between items.
segments.s647-g58: ز یزدان = “from God” (preposition + divine noun). همی‌خواستی is the past habitual/continuous form (with the prefix همی), 2nd-person singular past: “you kept asking/wanted.” زینهار here functions as a noun meaning “a warning, admonition” (object of خواستی). The clause thus says the addressee repeatedly sought a warning from God.
segments.s647-g59: برین گونه is an archaic/poetic expression meaning “in this way”: بر (on/upon) + the pronominal/orthographic ین (here functioning like این = “this”) + گونه (manner). تا هفت سال gives a duration “for/up to seven years” (تا = until/for + سال = year). از جهان = “from the world,” marking the domain referenced.
segments.s647-g60: ندیدند is past tense, 3rd-person plural negative: “they did not see.” سبزی is the direct object “greenness / verdure.” کهان و مهان are poetic nouns referring to the world/realm and the lands (archaic pair); they are coordinated with و. The whole clause: “they saw no greenness of the world or land.”
segments.s647-g61: به هشتم = “on the eighth” (به used here as a temporal preposition). بیامد is past tense “came/arrived.” مه (poetic spelling for ماه) here denotes the month, and فرودین names that month (Farvardīn). The phrase thus sets the time: “On the eighth [of Farvardin] the month came.”
segments.s647-g62: برآمد is an intransitive past verb “arose/appeared.” یکی is an indefinite determiner “one / a certain.” ابر = “cloud.” با + آفرین here means “with acclamation/praise” (با marking accompaniment); آفرین is used as an accompaniment meaning a cry of praise. The clause: “a cloud arose with an exclamation of praise.”
segments.s647-g63: همی before a past verb marks continued or habitual action; همی در بارید = “it kept raining” or “was raining.” بر خاک خشک is a locative phrase: بر = on/upon + خاک خشک = “dry earth” (noun followed by adjective). The clause describes ongoing rain falling on the dry ground.
segments.s647-g64: همی‌آمد again marks continuous action: “kept coming.” از بوستان = “from the garden” (از = from + بوستان = garden). بوی مشک = “the scent/smell of musk” (بوی + ماده). Word order is Persian: verb (continuous) then source phrase then the noun phrase naming the scent.
segments.s647-g273: Nominal phrase + instrumental phrase before the verb: همه گوشت بازو (all the flesh of the arm) is the direct object, then به دندان marks the instrument (“with his teeth”). The verb بکند is an archaic/imperfective narrative form (classical third-person) often rendered in English as a simple past or past habitual (“he bit”/“he would bite”). Word order (object–instrument–verb) is typical of Persian poetic diction.
segments.s647-g274: همی‌ریخت is an imperfective past/habitual narrative (“kept scattering/was casting”). بر تخت is a prepositional phrase “upon the throne.” خاک نژند is an epithet literally ‘base dust/earth’ that follows and characterizes تخت (the throne), so the clause means he was casting the flesh onto the throne, which he treated like base earth. The verb governs the preceding material and the direct object (the flesh) is implied to be what is being scattered.
segments.s647-g275: A coordinated subject phrase: سپاهی و شهری (“soldiers and townspeople”). ز ایران is a poetic preposition meaning “from/of Iran.” به درد here means “in sorrow/afflicted,” marking the emotional state that motivates the lament; the whole segment sets up who is stricken and why.
segments.s647-g276: A triple enumeration (زن و مرد و کودک) followed by the imperfective particle همی plus a deverbal noun مویه and the light verb کرد: همی مویه کرد = “kept lamenting” / “lamented.” کرد is the general past auxiliary/lexical verb here (literally “made/do” used to form the action). Word order places the people being named before the verbal phrase.
segments.s647-g277: This is a paired descriptive phrase. Each phrase begins with the distributive particle همه ("all/everyone") for emphasis. کنده‌موی is a compound adjective (کنده + موی) meaning literally "hair-torn" or figuratively "dishevelled/wild-haired," and خسته‌روی is another compound (خسته + روی) meaning "wearied-faced." و simply connects the two coordinated descriptions.
segments.s647-g278: A second paired phrase following the same pattern: همه repeats for distributive/emphatic effect. شاه‌جوی and راه‌جوی are compounds built with the suffix -جوی ("seeker of"); شاه‌جوی = "one seeking the king/court," راه‌جوی = "one seeking the road/way" (i.e. a traveler or wanderer). و connects the two coordinated roles.
segments.s647-g279: A subordinate temporal clause introduced by که which links this clause to the previous line. The sequence تا چون functions together as a temporal marker meaning roughly "when/so that when": تا provides a purposive/temporal nuance and چون here = "as/when." گریزند is the 3rd-person plural present (classical/poetic) describing "they flee," ز is the poetic preposition "from," and ایران‌زمین is the compound noun "land of Iran."
segments.s647-g280: This is a conditional clause. گر is the archaic/poetic conditional particle "if." آیند is the 3rd-person plural present subjunctive/indicative "they come" (here: "an army should come"). لشکر is the subject "army/host." ازان is an older fused form of از آن ("from that/from it") and links to the following noun phrase دشت کین, where دشت = "plain" and کین (postpositive) qualifies it as "of vengeance/hatred." The whole means "if an army comes from that plain of vengeance."
segments.s647-g249: همی‌راند is an archaic/imperfective verb form with the prefix همی- indicating repeated or continuous action (‘kept riding’). با introduces a circumstantial phrase of manner: با کام دل = ‘with the fulfillment (کام) of his heart (دل)’; خوشنواز is an adjectival/postpositive modifier meaning ‘pleasant/melodious,’ describing the state of that heart (i.e. his pleasure or pleasing disposition) and linking as manner to the verb.
segments.s647-g250: سرافراز is a predicate adjective describing the (same) subject as ‘proud/triumphant.’ با introduces accompaniment (‘with’), and لشکر رزمساز is a noun phrase in the instrumental/associative role — ‘an army that makes war’ (رزمساز = war‑making/instigating). Word order is adjectival + accompaniment: ‘triumphant with his war‑making host.’
segments.s647-g251: به تاراج دادن is a fixed verbal idiom meaning ‘to give over to pillage/plunder’; here داده is the past participle/past form ‘given’ and سپاه و بنه are the objects given (سپاه = army/host; بنه = bundles/baggage). به marks the direction/recipient of the giving (i.e. ‘to plunder’).
segments.s647-g252: نه ... و نه ... is the correlative negative ‘neither ... nor ...’. کس here functions as ‘anyone’ (negated by نه ...), دید is the past ‘saw/beheld’. میسره and میمنه name the two sides (here ‘left’ and ‘right’ in the battlefield sense); the clause says no one could see either side. (Note: میسره’s exact sense is archaic/idiomatic here and does not match the provided sense list.)
segments.s647-g253: ز is the poetic preposition ‘from’. چند is an indefinite numeral/adverb ‘a few/several’. بردند is the simple past ‘they took/carried off’. اسیر is the noun ‘captive’ and is the direct object: ‘they took several captives from the Iranians.’
segments.s647-g254: چه here introduces the following description of some of those captives (functioning roughly like ‘some/which’ — an archaic usage that does not map exactly to the listed senses). افگنده is a past participle ‘thrown/laid (down)’, بر خاک = ‘upon the ground’, و links the next description, خسته به تیر = ‘stricken/overcome (خسته) by arrows (به تیر)’ where به marks the instrument (‘by arrows’).
segments.s647-g255: نباید که باشد is an impersonal negative construction ‘it ought not to be that / there should not be’. جهانجوی is a compound noun ‘world‑seeker’ (i.e. an ambitious person seeking worldly gain). زفت (tar/pitch) is used metaphorically to describe the heart as morally foul; the clause means ‘there should not be an ambitious man whose heart is tar.’
segments.s647-g256: This line states the consequence/similarity in inverted poetic order: دل زفت = ‘a tarry heart’; با introduces the pairing/association, خاک تیره‌ست = ‘the earth is dark’ (تیره‌ست = predicate ‘is dark/obscure’), جفت = ‘mate/companion’ used predicatively to mean ‘is paired with’. Together: ‘a tarry heart is paired with dark earth’ (i.e. its fitting companion is the dark soil — burial/obscurity).
segments.s647-g9: Literal noun phrase with copula: سر ('chief, head') + مردمی ('of the people') describes a superlative/social position, followed by بردباری ('patience/forbearance') as the quality; بود is the past copula ('was'). Word order is nominal (predicate after the head) typical of classical Persian description.
segments.s647-g10: سبک‌سر (an adjective/noun meaning 'rash, light‑headed') is subject, همیشه ('always') is an adverb modifying the predicate, and به + خواری ('in humiliation/abasement') expresses a state; بود is the past copula. The construction 'به + noun' marks the state someone is in (poetic usage).
segments.s647-g11: A compact nominal clause: ستون ('pillar') + خرد ('wisdom') reads as 'the pillar of wisdom' (noun + genitive idea), then داد here functions as the noun 'justice' rather than the verb; و connects to the following item, and بخشایشست is a fused form with -ست (copula) meaning 'is forgiveness/mercy.' The copular -ست is an archaic/poetic present/past‑neutral 'is.'
segments.s647-g12: در بخشش ('in/among giving' — در marks the domain/state) + او را places 'he' as a topical/emphatic constituent (را used as topic marker here), چو ('like/as') introduces a simile, and آرایشست again uses the poetic copula -ست: 'is an ornament.' The phrase thus means 'in his giving he is like an ornament.'
segments.s647-g13: زبان چرب is a noun + adjective phrase ('a flattering/smooth tongue'), joined by و to گویندگی ('eloquence/style of speech'); فر اوست puts فر ('glory/splendour') before the pronoun and copula (a common poetic word order) to state 'these are his glory' — اوست = 'is his.'
segments.s647-g14: دلیری و مردانگی form a compound subject ('bravery and manliness'); پر ('full (of)') precedes the pronoun او and is followed by the copula ست, yielding the sense 'he is full of bravery and manliness.' The adjective 'پر' here takes the idea of being 'full (of X)' and the unusual order (adjective before pronoun) is poetic inversion.
segments.s647-g15: هران (a contraction of هر آن, 'each of those' / 'those') + نامور ('renowned') describes the people in question; کو here functions in an interrogative/relative sense (classical usage) introducing the clause 'who/that do not have'; ندارد is the present/past‑neutral verb 'does not have' (present form used in narrative), and خرد is the noun 'wisdom.' Together: 'those famed who do not possess wisdom.'
segments.s647-g16: ز ('from', poetic preposition) + تخت بزرگی ('throne of greatness' — تخت 'throne' qualified by بزرگی 'greatness/grandeur') sets the scene/source, and کجا برخورد؟ is a rhetorical question 'where will (they) meet/encounter (it)?' برخورد here means 'encounter/come upon' and the question asks how those without wisdom can attain or encounter the high throne.
segments.s647-g33: شهنشاه ایران is the subject phrase ('the Shah of Iran'). چو here is the temporal conjunction 'when' introducing a subordinate clause; دید is the simple past 'saw' (verb + past tense). آن شگفت follows the verb as the direct object and reads as a noun phrase 'that wonder/that marvelous thing.'
segments.s647-g34: خراج و گزیت are coordinated objects ('the tax/tribute and Gazit'); برگرفت is the past of the compound verb بر-گرفتن and here means 'took away/removed' (note the separable prefix بر-). از جهان is a prepositional phrase 'from the world/realm' indicating the source from which they were removed.
segments.s647-g35: به هر سو 'to every direction/place' is the locative frame; که introduces a relative/locative clause 'where'; انبار بودش is 'a storehouse was (there)' with بودش the past copula plus enclitic 'it was'; نهان is an adjective/participle 'hidden' qualifying the storehouse.
segments.s647-g36: ببخشید is the past tense of بخشیدن used here in the active sense 'gave/bestowed'; بر (here 'to/toward') marks the recipients; کهتران و مهان names the classes receiving the gifts ('the lesser/poor and the guests'). The verb thus distributes whatever was found to these groups.
segments.s647-g37: خروشی برآمد is a noun + past verb 'a commotion/uproar arose'; ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; درگاه شاه is the genitive phrase 'the court/threshold of the king' serving as the source of the sound.
segments.s647-g38: که‌ای is a vocative/interjectional form (classical poetic usage akin to 'O ...'); نامداران is the addressee 'the renowned/illustrious ones'; با دستگاه follows as 'with (their) retinue/court,' describing who is being addressed. Together this is an exclamatory address from the court.
segments.s647-g39: غله هرچ دارید is an imperative context where 'غله' (grain) is the theme and هرچ دارید 'whatever you have' modifies it; پیدا کنید is an imperative verbal phrase literally 'make (it) appear/bring (it) forth' — پیدا used here with کنید expresses an order to produce or present the grain.
segments.s647-g40: ز دینار پیروز is a prepositional phrase 'with/from the dinars of the victorious' specifying the coinage/wealth to be used; گنج آگنید is an imperative instruction using آگنید (from آگند/آگندن) meaning 'fill (the) treasure' — i.e. 'fill the treasury with those dinars.'
segments.s647-g241: برآورد is a past-tense, 3rd-person singular form used here in a narrational/poetic sense — 'caused/produced/threw up'. زان is a prepositional adverb 'from that'. کنده functions as a noun 'the dug-out place / the uprooting' (here the location/act referred to by زان). هر کس که زیست is a relative phrase 'everyone who lived' (هر کس = 'every person', که introduces the relative clause, زیست = past of 'to live'). The overall word order is verb — prepositional phrase — quantified subject with relative clause, a common poetic ordering.
segments.s647-g242: همان is a demonstrative/adjectival 'that very / the same'. خاک = 'earth/soil' as the subject. بر is the preposition 'upon/on'. بخت here means 'fate/fortune'. ایشان is the 3rd-person plural pronoun 'they/their'. گریست is the past tense 'wept' (3rd-person singular), used impersonally here with خاک as the subject: 'the earth wept for their fate'.
segments.s647-g243: بزرگان و پیکارجویان is a coordinated noun phrase 'the great ones and the fighters/warriors'. هران follows the coordinated noun and is distributive — 'each of them' / 'each one' — a poetic/postpositive form that distributes the following action over the members of the group.
segments.s647-g244: کسی را is the focused/definite object/topic 'anyone / whoever' marked by the object marker را; که introduces the relative clause 'who'. در + کنده = 'into/in the hollow' (در as locative), آمد is past tense 'came/entered'. زمان at the end is temporal 'at that time' modifying the whole clause. Together the chunk reads as a relative/temporal clause: 'who came into the hollow at that time.'
segments.s647-g245: شکسته is a past participle/adjective 'broken' placed before the nouns it qualifies. سر و پشت is a coordinated noun phrase 'head and back'. پیروزشاه is a compound/title 'the victorious king' (a name or honorific); grammatically it attaches to سر و پشت as the possessor/identifier. The structure yields 'the head and back of the victorious king were broken.'
segments.s647-g246: شه نامداران is a nominal phrase 'the king of the renowned / the renowned king' (شه = 'king', نامداران = 'renowned ones' used attributively). با introduces an instrument/attendant phrase 'with', here with تاج 'crown'. و گاه links another noun (گاه = 'time/occasion/stature' here functioning as part of a poetic attribute — 'crown and rank/seat'). The phrase lists honorific trappings of the king.
segments.s647-g247: ز شاهان introduces the domain 'of the kings / among the kings'. نبد is an archaic negative past form meaning 'there was not / there remained not' (negative of بودن in poetical registers). زنده is the predicate 'alive'. جز introduces the exception 'except', and کیقباد is the name 'Kay Qobad'. Together: 'Among the kings none remained alive except Kay Qobad.'
segments.s647-g248: شد is past 'became' / 'happened'. آن لشکر و پادشاهی is the compound subject 'that army and kingship/kingdom'. به باد is an idiomatic expression 'to the wind' meaning 'ruined/utterly lost'. So the clause means 'that army and kingdom were swept away / came to ruin.'
segments.s647-g201: چنین (an adverb) precedes the verb and means 'thus/so'. گفت is the past tense verb 'said' (poetic narrator speech). کای is the vocative particle 'O' addressing someone directly; داور داد و پاک is a noun phrase in apposition to that vocative: داور 'judge' qualified by the nouns داد 'justice' and پاک 'pure' (joined by و); the whole phrase is a poetic vocative 'O Judge of justice and purity.'
segments.s647-g202: تویی is the emphatic copula 'thou art' (present tense 'you are') used for direct address. آفریننده is the noun 'creator' and takes the following nouns as its objects in a genitive/compound sense. هور و خاک is a coordinated noun phrase 'light/splendor and earth' specifying what is created.
segments.s647-g203: تو دانی که is a subordinating clause opener: تو 'you' + دانی 'know' + که 'that'. پیروز is an adjective/noun 'victorious' and modifies or names the person; بیدادگر is an epithet 'oppressor/tyrant' attached to that person — together 'the victorious tyrant'.
segments.s647-g204: This is a comparative clause: ز بهرام بیشی ندارد هنر literally 'from Bahram more does not have skill' — i.e. 'he has no more skill than Bahram.' ز is the poetic comparative preposition 'than/from', بیشی is the comparative 'more/greater', ندارد is the present negative 'does not have', and هنر 'skill/art' is the thing compared.
segments.s647-g205: پی او 'پی' (literary) + او = 'after him' or 'in pursuit of him'; ز روی زمین 'from/upon the surface of the earth' (ز indicating movement from or across); برگسل here is a poetic/archaic verbal or verbalized image meaning to burst forth/tear away/move (the exact nuance is archaic and compressed in poetry), forming the action of pursuit across the earth.
segments.s647-g206: مه before each noun is an archaic/poetic intensifier (here functioning like 'great' or an emphatic possessive/intensive element). نیرو 'strength', آهنگ جانش 'آهنگ' (intention/aim/motion) with the possessive suffix -ش ('his') = 'the aim/movement of his life', and دل 'heart'. The sequence lists these forces/emotions in compact, parallel noun phrases.
segments.s647-g207: سخن‌های بیداد is a genitive phrase 'words/utterances of oppression' (سخن‌های plural + بیداد 'oppression/tyranny'). گوید همی is the classical poetic present/habitual construction: گوید 'he says' with the particle همی indicating continuous or habitual action ('he keeps saying' / 'he continually utters').
segments.s647-g208: بزرگی (greatness) appears as the object, followed by به شمشیر where به indicates instrument/means 'by/with the sword'. جوید همی is another present/habitual verb phrase 'he seeks/seeks after' with همی again marking ongoing action: 'he continually seeks greatness by the sword.'
segments.s647-g89: بلاش is the subject (proper name). از بر تخت is a compact locative phrase (از + بر + تخت) meaning 'on/at the throne' in poetic registration; تخت is the place noun and از/بر together indicate position. بنشست is simple past ('he sat down') and شاد is a postverbal predicate/adverbial describing his state ('happily').
segments.s647-g90: که introduces a reason/explanatory clause ('for' / 'that'). کهتر is an adjective meaning 'younger' modifying پسر 'son'. بود is the past copula linking the subject to the predicate. با + مهر و داد is a predicate with با marking accompaniment/quality ('with affection and justice'), where مهر = 'affection/love' and داد = 'justice'.
segments.s647-g91: یکی پارسی is an indefinite noun phrase ('a certain Persian') with یکی preposing the noun. بود is past copula 'there was / he was'. بس here is an intensifier ('very' / 'much') modifying the following adjective نامدار 'renowned' (postnominal adjective), a typical classical Persian ordering.
segments.s647-g92: ورا is an object/indirect-object-style clitic/word meaning 'to him'. سوفزا is a proper name here (uncommon/marked sense). خواندی is past tense of خواندن used causatively/denotatively 'called/named' (one of the standard uses of خواند). شهریار is the agent 'the king', placed after the verb by poetic inversion.
segments.s647-g93: بفرمود is a polite/honorific past verb meaning 'he commanded/said'. پیروز is the subject (proper name) following the verb by poetic word order. کایدر appears as the addressee or name invoked in the command (here treated as a vocative/proper name). بباش is an imperative form 'be/stand' (a directive), used to issue the order.
segments.s647-g94: چو begins a causal/comparative clause here ('as' / 'for'). دستور شایسته is a noun phrase 'a fitting/appropriate order'. نزد بلاش means 'in the presence of Balash' or 'before Balash', with نزد marking the locus/recipient. The whole segment gives the reason/setting for the command.
segments.s647-g95: سپه را places 'the army' as a definite object/topic with را (object marker). سوی جنگ ترکان is a directional phrase 'toward the war with the Turks' (سوی = 'toward', جنگ ترکان = 'the Turks' war'). کشید is the past tense of کشیدن here meaning 'he led/drew/marched (the army)'; word order is object + direction + verb, common in Persian narrative.
segments.s647-g96: همی is a narrative particle here ('thus/thereby' / emphatic). تاج و تخت کیی را is the definite nominal object 'the Kayanian crown and throne' (را marks them). سزید is past/intransitive 'it befitted' / 'was due' / 'became deserved'—the subject is understood from context (he/the action just described).
segments.s647-g145: من اینک بیارم is a first-person present (classical narrative) clause: من = 'I', اینک = 'now', بیارم = 'I bring' (classical form of بیاورم). The verb here presents the speaker's immediate action. سپاهی گران follows as the direct object: سپاهی 'army/host' modified by the adjective گران 'heavy/mighty/valuable'. Word order is subject — temporal adverb — verb — object phrase (the verb comes before its object in this line for poetic word order).
segments.s647-g146: سرافراز گردان جنگ آوران is a noun phrase further describing that army: سرافراز is an adjective 'proud/triumphant', گردان = 'troop/unit' (here a unit of men), and جنگ‌آوران is a plural compound 'war-bringers' = 'warriors/fighters'. The three words stack as adjective + head noun + genitive/compound describing the troop's character.
segments.s647-g147: نمانم مگر سایه خوشنواز: نمانم is the first-person negative of ماندن 'I do not / will not remain' (classical negative present). مگر here means 'except' (introducing what little will remain). سایه خوشنواز is the exception: سایه 'a shade/shadow' modified by خوشنواز 'pleasant-sounding/pleasant' used adjectivally ('a pleasant shade'). The clause says the speaker will not remain except in that minimal form.
segments.s647-g148: که باشد بروی زمین بر دراز is a relative/adverbial clause modifying 'سایه': که = 'that/which', باشد is the present/subjunctive form of بودن here with sense 'may be / is' (classical usage), بروی (literally 'go/lie on') governs زمین 'the earth/ground' with the preposition بر = 'on/upon', and دراز = 'long'. Together: 'that may be long upon the earth' (i.e. rest long on the ground). Word order reflects classical/poetic syntax with the verb before its complements.
segments.s647-g149: فرستاده آمد = 'the messenger came/arrived' (simple past narrative). به کردار گرد is an adverbial phrase: به + کردار 'in/with manner' and گرد 'about/around' (here adverbial), so the phrase means 'moving about in his manner' or 'going about as was his custom'. The clause describes how the messenger presented himself.
segments.s647-g150: شنیده سخن‌ها همه یاد کرد is a compound predicate: شنیده is a past participle 'having heard', followed by the object سخن‌ها 'the words/speeches' and همه 'all'. یاد کرد is a light-verb construction 'made remembrance' = 'recounted/remembered'. So: 'Having heard, he recounted all the words.' The sequence places the participial circumstance before the main verb, typical in classical Persian narrative.
segments.s647-g151: همی‌گفت یک چند با خوشنواز: همی‌گفت is an iterative or habitual past 'he kept saying / he kept speaking'. یک چند here functions adverbially as 'for a while' or 'a short time/a few (words)'. با خوشنواز = 'with the pleasant-singer/musician' (با + noun = 'with X'). The clause means he conversed for a while with the musician.
segments.s647-g152: ازان شاه گردنکش و دیرساز: ازان = 'of/from that' (introducing the topic), شاه = 'the king', گردنکش 'neck-breaking' literally but idiomatically 'rebellious / headstrong', و = 'and', دیرساز literally 'one who makes delay' = 'slow to act / obstinate' in this context. Together they form a short appositive description: 'that king, headstrong and slow-to-act.'
segments.s647-g209: به گرد سپه is a prepositional phrase meaning 'around/about the host' (به + گرد). The clause بر یکی کنده کرد has the verbal compound کنده کرد (کنده as verbal adjective/participle + کرد as the past tense auxiliary) and means 'he pulled/removed (one) upon/from one'. Word order places the locative phrase before the verb, and کرد is the past tense main verb.
segments.s647-g210: سرش را is the direct object (سر + enclitic possessive ش + object marker را). بپوشید is a past action 'he covered/put over' (پوشیدن in past form; the initial ب- occurs in classical narrative forms). The coordination و آگنده کرد links a second verb phrase: آگنده (adjective 'full, filled') + کرد (causative/performative auxiliary) = 'he made (it) full' or 'he filled (it)'. Both verbs are narrative past actions referring back to the same subject.
segments.s647-g211: کمندی فزون بود is a verbless descriptive clause with the copula بود ('was'): کمندی (subject 'a noose/snare') modified by the adjective فزون ('larger/more'). بالای اوی is a locative phrase 'above him' (بالا + -ی + اوی, an archaic 3rd-person pronoun). The sequence follows the classical order Subject – Predicate – Locative.
segments.s647-g212: همان is the demonstrative 'that/the same'. سی ارش کرده is an adjectival phrase where سی ارش ('thirty arsh' — arsh = a traditional unit of length) is understood together with کرده (past participle/verb-form used adjectivally) to mean 'made/amounting to thirty arsh'. پهنای اوی is a genitive/possessive phrase 'its width' (پهنای + اوی), so overall 'that same (one) was thirty arsh in width.'
segments.s647-g213: چو این کرده شد is a temporal clause ('when this had been done'); چو functions as 'when', کرده شد is a perfect/result construction. نام یزدان بخواند is the main clause: نام یزدان ('the name of God') followed by بخواند — here used as a narrative past meaning 'he called/invoked'. The verb appears in the classical narrative form.
segments.s647-g214: ز پیش سمرقند is a locative/prepositional phrase 'from before/in front of Samarkand' (ز = poetic 'from', پیش = 'before/in front of'). لشکر براند is the verb phrase 'he drove the army away' (راند/براند as past-narrative 'drove off'). Word order places the place phrase before the verbal action.
segments.s647-g215: وزان is an archaic fused particle (و + ز آن) meaning 'and from that / thereafter'. روی سرگشته is a nominal phrase 'his face (being) bewildered/confused' (سرگشته as participial adjective). پیروز شاه is the head noun phrase 'the victorious king' (adjective + noun). The whole phrase sets the subject and its accompanying descriptive phrases before the verb.
segments.s647-g216: همی‌راند is a classical continuous/habitual past form ('he was driving' or 'he drove (with continuing force)'); the prefix همی- often marks continuous aspect in poetic-narrative Persian. چون باد is a simile 'like the wind'. لشکر به راه places the object + direction 'the army on the road/along the way'. The clause thus reads as a continuous-past action with a simile.
segments.s647-g73: This clause is a nominative phrase + verb: two nouns in apposition (جهاندار 'world‑ruler' and گوینده 'the one speaking') followed by the past verb گفت 'said'. این ری‌ست is a direct statement quoted by that verb. Note that گفت is the reporting verb in past tense, and ری‌ست is an archaic copular phrase (see next segment).
segments.s647-g74: که introduces a relative/locative clause 'which/where'. آرام (preverbal adjective) modifies شاهان (kings); the predicate is the fused poetic/copular form فرخ‌پی‌ست — an older compound copula meaning something like 'is fortunate/auspicious'. Word order here (adj + noun + predicate) is a literary inversion common in epic poetry.
segments.s647-g75: دگر is an adverb meaning 'again' or 'in addition'; کرد is the simple past 'did/made/gave' and here governs the following instrumental phrase بادان ('with that' / 'thereby') plus the object پیروز نام ('the name Pīrūz'). The verb + instrumental + object order is a poetic variation on SOV.
segments.s647-g76: خنیده is the perfect/passive participle of خواندن in the sense 'was sung/was praised' (here functioning as a passive/resultive). به هرجایش is an adverbial phrase 'in/throughout every place' (به + هرجایش). آرام و کام are two nouns/adjectives joined by و: 'peace (calm) and fulfillment/satisfaction.' Together they describe the manner or state in which he was celebrated.
segments.s647-g77: که begins a relative/explanatory clause. اکنونش is اکنون with the clitic -ش ('now it'), functioning as the logical subject/topic; خوانی (from خواندن) here means 'is called' (they call it) and همی is an old continuous/affirmative particle 'now/indeed/still'. The city name اردبیل is the predicate/name assigned.
segments.s647-g78: که introduces a second explanatory clause 'for/since'. قیصر is the subject (the emperor), بدو is the prepositional/clitic form 'to it' or 'toward it', دارد is the present indicative 'has/holds', and از داد میل is an ablative/causal phrase 'a liking/affection because of (its) justice' (از ... میل = 'inclination due to justice').
segments.s647-g79: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. این بوم‌ها is the plural noun phrase 'these lands/regions'. یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/wholly' modifying آباد (prosperous/inhabited), and کرد is the past verb 'made/caused'. The clause is a prototypical temporal subordinate ('when X happened').
segments.s647-g80: دل مردم is a genitive/possessive phrase 'the hearts of the people'. پر خرد is an adjective construction where پر 'full (of)' takes خرد 'wisdom' as its complement. The final شاد کرد is a causative past construction 'made (them) happy.' Together: 'he made the people's wise hearts glad.'
segments.s647-g1: بیامد is a past-tense verb 'he came' (simple past). به + تخت denotes motion/position 'to the throne' (به = to; تخت = throne). کیی is an epithet 'Kayanian' (the Kayanid dynasty); it modifies تخت. برنشست is a past verb meaning 'sat upon / took his seat' (بر- prefix + نشست). The segment is a compact past narrative: subject implied 'he' + two successive actions (came, then sat).
segments.s647-g2: چنان چون is a poetic emphatic simile 'just as / such as'. بود is the past copula 'was'. شاه is the noun 'king' and یزدان‌پرست is a compound adjective 'God‑worshipping / pious', so the clause states the manner/quality of the sitting-king: 'as a pious king was'. Note the verbless quality phrase follows the copula.
segments.s647-g3: نخستین 'first' (adverb/adjective) and چنین 'thus/so' set the manner and order. گفت here is the verb 'said/spoke' (poetic past); با 'with' introduces the company, مهتران = 'the elders/chief men' (poetic plural). Word order: adverb/manner + verb + with‑phrase showing interlocutors.
segments.s647-g4: که‌ای is a poetic vocative construction (equivalent to که ای → 'O ...') used to address the addressees. پرهنر 'learned/cultured' and پاکدل 'pure‑hearted' are coordinating epithets describing the audience. سروران is the plural of 'lord'/'noble' and is the vocative addressee. The phrase is direct address: 'O cultured, pure‑hearted lords'.
segments.s647-g5: همی‌خواهم is a present/future-leaning desiderative 'I want / I ask' — the 'همی-' prefix gives an ongoing/intentional sense. از is the preposition 'from/of' introducing the source (here 'from the Judge' = asking of God). داور in this context is the divine adjudicator, i.e., 'the Judge' (here meaning God). بی‌نیاز describes that Judge as 'self-sufficient / needing nothing' (an epithet of deity).
segments.s647-g6: که is a subordinating particle 'that' introducing the desired clause. باشد is the jussive/subjunctive form 'may be / be' expressing the petition. مرا is the accusative/genitive clitic 'to me / for me' (marks the experiencer). را here is not an object marker but appears as part of the clitic sequence in the supplied segmentation (the intended meaning is governed by مرا); زندگانی 'life' and دراز 'long' form the predicate 'a long life'. The clause literally: 'that there be for me a long life.'
segments.s647-g7: The repeated که is a poetic resumptive/continuative 'that/so that' and introduces another wish‑clause. که ... را به که دارم is an elliptical sequence; the first cluster (که که را به که دارم) is a compressed poetic construction meaning roughly 'so that I may have (it) to whom I should' — here functioning as a rhetorical intensifier rather than a literalwho/whom question. و connects the clauses. مه به مه is an adverbial repetition 'month by month' (مه here used as 'month' in poetic diction). Note: the repeated interrogative particles function idiomatically in older Persian poetry.
segments.s647-g8: فراوان 'plentifully/abundant' modifies خرد 'wisdom' (here خرد = 'wisdom, understanding'). باشدم is the optative/subjunctive first‑person 'may I be / I might be' (poetic form with -م). روز به 'day by' (روز به روز) sets the temporal rhythm 'day by day' — here the second particle (به) is left to be continued in the next line, but functions adverbially. Overall: 'may I be abundant in wisdom, day by day.'
segments.s647-g49: کسی‌گر is an archaic conditional 'if anyone' (کسی + enclitic گر). بمیرد is the classical subjunctive/present form of مردن used in conditional clauses ('dies' / 'should die'). بنایافت is an archaic past/passive-type verb meaning 'was found/there was found' and takes نان ('bread') as its object; the verb comes after the conditional clause, giving the sense 'if X happens, Y is found.'
segments.s647-g50: ز ... و از ... mark sources: ز برنا = 'from the young' and از پیر مرد و زنان = 'and from old men and women.' Note the two prepositions (ز and از) both indicate origin; word order is noun (برنا / پیر مرد و زنان) following the preposition without an explicit ezāfe, typical of poetic diction.
segments.s647-g51: بریزم is a first-person singular present/subjunctive used futurally or optatively ('I will spill / I shall pour'); the verb comes first for emphasis. ز تن is a prepositional phrase 'from the body' marking source, and خون + انباردار form 'the blood of the storekeeper' with the possessor (انباردار) following the noun without an ezāfe in this poetic register.
segments.s647-g52: کجا introduces a rhetorical question 'where?'. کار یزدان is a genitive construction 'the work/deed of God' with the possessor after the head noun. گرفتست is an archaic contracted perfect ('گرفته‌ست' = 'has been taken/has become'), and خوار is a predicate adjective 'despised/low' — together 'has the work of God been made despicable?'.
segments.s647-g53: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense 'he ordered/said'. تا introduces a subordinate clause meaning 'that/so that' (not temporal here). خانه is the object 'house', and بگذاشتند is past plural 'they placed/set up' — the clause means 'he ordered that they set up the house.'
segments.s647-g54: به دشت آمد is a simple past 'came into/to the plain' (به + place + آمد). دست برداشتند is an idiom 'they withdrew their hands' meaning they ceased action or assistance; here دست is literal 'hand' used idiomatically and برداشتند is past plural 'they took away/withdrew.' The two clauses are joined by و 'and.'
segments.s647-g55: همی is a classical adverb often marking continuous or emphatic action ('kept / continually / straightway'); here it intensifies the verb. به آسمان اندر آمد literally 'came into the sky' — an elevated poetic way to say 'rose to the heavens.' خروش is the noun 'cry/uproar' serving as the subject of the clause.
segments.s647-g56: ز بس introduces a cause or measure 'from so much / because of so great' (بس here is an intensifier). The following series مویه و درد و زاری و جوش lists sources of the tumult: mourning (مویه), sorrow/pain (درد) in an emotional sense, lamentation (زاری), and جوش as 'surge/boiling' (emotional tumult). The repeated و simply strings the nouns together.
segments.s647-g105: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. پیروز شیراوژن is the personal name (subject) placed before the verb. آنجا is a locative adverb 'there'. رسید is the simple past (3rd sg.) 'arrived/reached' and completes the clause 'When Pīrūz Shīrāvozhan arrived there.'
segments.s647-g106: This is the main clause whose verb is بدید ('he saw', past 3rd sg.). نشان here is a noun 'sign/standard' and کردن functions as a light verb forming a verbal-noun phrase (the act/display of a sign) or simply part of a terse classical noun-verb compound; شاه ایران ('the King of Iran') is the possessor phrase modifying the sign. Together: 'he saw the (royal) sign/standard of the King of Iran.'
segments.s647-g107: چنین گفت = 'he said thus/so' (چنین = thus, گفت = said). یکسر is an adverb 'entirely/at once/straightaway' modifying the speech, and به + گردنکشان is the prepositional object 'to the گردنکشان' (literally 'neck-stretchers' — here 'proud ones' or 'rebels'), marking the addressees.
segments.s647-g108: که introduces a subordinate clause ('that'). از پیش ترکان literally 'from the earlier Turks' = 'among/former to the Turks' (پیش = before/previous). بر + ین is here a poetic combination forming برین 'this/so great' attached to همنشان 'counterpart/match' — the clause asserts there is no comparable match among the earlier Turks. This is elliptical classical phrasing typical of Shahnameh.
segments.s647-g109: مناره برآرم is a first-person volitional construction: برآرم (present/subjunctive form with بر‑) here expresses 'I will raise' or 'I will set up' (a vow-like intention). به شمشیر و گنج uses به instrumentally ('by/with sword and treasure') specifying means.
segments.s647-g110: ز هیتال 'from Hītāl' gives source/material. تا introduces a purposive/result clause ('so that' / 'in order that'). کس نباشد به رنج is a negative present-subjunctive construction meaning 'there shall be no one in suffering' (کس here functions as 'anyone' in a negative sense 'no one'; نباشد is the negative subjunctive/optative of 'to be'; به رنج = 'in hardship/suffering').
segments.s647-g111: چو باشد is a temporal clause 'when (it) is' (باشد is the present/subjunctive form of 'to be' used narratively). مناره is the subject 'the minaret'. به پیش = 'in front of / before' and برک is a noun (here likely 'berak' meaning prosperity/pond/blessing depending on context) marking the location or thing before which the minaret stands.
segments.s647-g112: بزرگان (the great ones) is the subject; به پیش من is the prepositional phrase 'before me / in my presence' (به + پیش + من). آرند is 3rd‑person plural present/subjunctive 'they bring' (آر- stem = 'bring'). چک is the object 'a drop' or 'a small tribute' — here 'they will bring (me) tribute.'
segments.s647-g65: شده is a past participle/compound perfect-like form meaning ‘had become/was’ and here introduces the resultant state. ژاله بر گل is a prepositional phrase ‘dew on the flower(s)’ (ژاله = dew; بر = on; گل = flower). چو introduces a simile ‘like/as’, and مل در قدح is a descriptive phrase ‘a realm/kingdom in a goblet’ (مل modified by the locative در قدح). The whole segment describes the appearance of the dew using a poetic simile.
segments.s647-g66: همی‌تافت is an imperfective/continuous verb form (همی‑ prefix + تافت) meaning ‘kept shining/gleamed’ — the همی emphasizes ongoing action in poetic diction. از introduces source (‘from/out of’), and ابر قوس قزح is a noun cluster ‘the cloud (from which) the arch of the rainbow’ — قوس قزح functions as ‘rainbow‑arc’. The verb governs the following prepositional phrase describing origin.
segments.s647-g67: زمانه is a personified noun here (‘time/fortune’). برست is an older past/intransitive verb meaning ‘was removed/ceased’ (i.e., ‘was rid of’); از marks the source/cause ‘from’. بد بدگمان is a collocation meaning ‘evil/ill suspicion’ or ‘ill‑trust’ (بد qualifying بدگمان). The clause says that the era/fortune was freed from that bad suspicion.
segments.s647-g68: به هرجای is an adverbial prepositional phrase ‘to/at every place/wherever’. بر زه نهاده is a participial construction: نهاده (past participle of نهادن, ‘to place’) with the prepositional بر + زه ‘on the bowstring’, describing a state already achieved. کمان (bow) completes the phrase; word order here is compact/poetic but reads as ‘the bowstring had been set on the bow everywhere’. The verb is implicit in the participial adjective نهاده.
segments.s647-g69: چو here = ‘when’. پیروز is the subject ‘the victor’. ازان روز تنگی is a genitival/time phrase ‘from that day's narrowness/distress’ (ازان = ‘from that’, روز = ‘day’, تنگی = ‘distress/oppression’). برست is the intransitive past meaning ‘was removed/ceased’. Together: ‘when the victor was relieved of that day's distress’.
segments.s647-g70: The first بر + آرام gives an adverbial sense ‘in/with calmness’ or ‘in peace’. The second بر + تخت شاهی is the prepositional phrase ‘upon the royal throne’ (تخت = throne, شاهی here adjectival ‘royal’). نشست is a past tense verb ‘sat’ (or ‘took a seat’). Word order places the adverbial notion before the prepositional location and the verb concludes the clause.
segments.s647-g71: یکی is an indefinite/introducing word ‘a certain’ / ‘one’, here functioning to open the clause. شارستان کرد is V‑O ‘made/built a town/city’ (کرد = did/made). پیروز modifies the subject agent ‘the victor’ and کام (‘desire/wish’) gives the purpose or satisfaction: ‘the victor built a city to fulfill his desire’. The compact, somewhat inverted word order is typical of epic/poetic diction.
segments.s647-g72: بفرمود is an honorific/polite past tense ‘he said/commanded’. کو is the interrogative ‘where’. را is the object/definiteness marker attached here to emphasize the question word (classical/poetic placement). نهادند is past‑plural ‘they placed’ (نهادن), and نام is the direct object ‘name’. Together the line reports his question/command: ‘Where did they place the name?’ (i.e., what name did they give or where was the name placed).
segments.s647-g233: به + noun + در is a locative phrasing: به here means “towards/into,” followed by the place (کنده) and the locative postposition در, then the past verb افتاد “fell/was thrown.” با introduces an accompaniment phrase (“with”), چند is an indefinite numeral/adjective “several,” and مرد is the accompanying noun “men.” Word order is somewhat compacted for poetic effect but functions as: “(he) fell into the pit with several men.”
segments.s647-g234: This is a coordinated apposition that describes those accompanying him: بزرگان “the great ones” and شیران (literally “lions,” used metaphorically for brave men). روز نبرد is a temporal/attributive phrase meaning “the day of battle” (روز + noun that specifies the kind of day). The whole segment qualifies the men mentioned before: “the great and the lions of the battle‑day.”
segments.s647-g235: چو is the subordinating conjunction “when.” نرسی here functions as an arrival/arrivalive sense (“(he) arrived / Nersi arrived”) or as the proper name Nersi in context; برادرش is “his brother” with the enclitic ـش marking possession. The following و links the next proper name فرخ قباد (Farrokh Qobad). The phrase therefore lists persons: “when Nersi, his brother, and Farrokh Qobad (came).”
segments.s647-g236: This is an appositive/group description: بزرگان “the great ones” and شاهان “kings”; فرخ نژاد is a compound modifier meaning “of fortunate/auspicious lineage” (فرخ modifying نژاد). The whole phrase characterizes the people named in the previous segment: “the great men and kings of the fortunate stock.”
segments.s647-g237: برین سان is a fixed classical phrase meaning “in this manner/thus” (بر + این/ین + سان). نگون شد is a passive/resultative phrase: نگون “overthrown/laid low” with شد “became/was” (past auxiliary) yielding “was overthrown/brought low.” سر هفت شاه places the affected noun phrase “the heads of seven kings” before the predicate, a common poetic word order: “the heads of seven kings were brought low in this way.”
segments.s647-g238: همه begins the next clause element “all/everyone”; نامداران is a plural adjective/noun meaning “renowned ones/famed men.” زرین is an adjective “golden” modifying کلاه “helmet/hat.” Together this nominal group describes the fallen as “all the famed, with golden helmets.” The noun-adjective ordering (زرین کلاه) is standard poetic adjacency.
segments.s647-g239: وزان is a compact classical prepositional/relational phrase meaning “and from that” (و + زان). جایگه means “place/spot.” شاددل and خوشنواز are epithets describing a person: شاددل “cheerful/joyful” and خوشنواز “melodious” or “a skilled musician” — together they form the subject phrase “the cheerful, melodious (one) from that place.”
segments.s647-g240: به نزدیکی is a prepositional phrase meaning “to/into the vicinity (nearness).” کنده is the place-word repeated here (the pit/dug place). آمد is the past tense verb “came.” فراز is an adverbial/poetic postposition meaning “up/forward/on high,” placed after آمد for elevated poetic effect: “came up to the vicinity of the pit.”
segments.s647-g25: دگر سال = “another year,” with the temporal phrase fronted for emphasis (دگر = another). رویِ هوا is a possessive/nominal compound meaning “the face/surface of the air” (روی = face/surface + هوا = air). خشک شد is a past-tense/resultative construction: خشک (adjective) + شد (past of شدن) = “became dry.”
segments.s647-g26: به marks location/into here (≈ “in/into”), followed by a genitive chain جو اندرون آب = “the water inside the barley” (جو = barley, اندرون = inner/inside, آب = water). چون introduces a simile “like,” مشک is the thing compared to (musk), and شد is the past “became.” The whole clause: “the water within the barley became like musk.”
segments.s647-g27: سه دیگر همان = “three others/the next three (were) the same,” with عدد (سه) + دیگر indicating additional items; همان repeated means “the same” or “likewise.” و چهارم همان attaches the fourth in the same way (“and the fourth likewise”). The clause is a terse, poetic enumeration of further years/events being identical.
segments.s647-g28: ز خشکی = “because of the dryness/drought” (ز used poetically for سبب = because of). نبد is an archaic/poetic negative past form of بودن (≈ نبُود) meaning “was not / there was not.” The structure renders “نبد هیچکس شادمان” = “there was no one joyful,” i.e. “no one was happy.” Note the verb precedes the subject and predicate follows, a common poetic inversion.
segments.s647-g29: هوا را is topical/definite (را here marks the air as topic: “as for the air” or “the air’s…”). دهان = “mouth/opening,” so هوا را دهان = “the air’s mouth.” خشک چون خاک شد is “became dry like dust” (چون = like, خاک = dust/earth), with شد marking the resultant state.
segments.s647-g30: ز تنگی = “because of scarcity/shortage” (ز = because of). به جو آب = a locative/genitive chain meaning “in the barley, the water” (به marking location/into, جو = barley, آب = water). تریاک شد = “became opium” (a figurative simile: the scant water turned into something like opium), with شد as the past/resultative verb.
segments.s647-g31: ز بس مردن مردم و چارپای = “because of the great number of deaths of people and quadrupeds.” بس here intensifies the following verbal noun مردن (“dying”), i.e. “so much/so many deaths.” مردم و چارپای are the groups affected (people and four-footed animals).
segments.s647-g32: پیی را ندیدند = “they did not see a trace/track” (پیی is a poetic word for track/trace/pursuit; را marks the definite object). بر خاک جای = “on the ground/earth” (بر = on/upon, خاک = earth, جای = place), specifying where no trace was seen. ندیدند is simple past, 3rd-person plural.
segments.s647-g217: This noun phrase describes a person by a sequence of modifiers: وزین روی (a grave/dignified face) + پر بیم دل (having a heart full of fear) + خوشنواز (the minstrel/one skilled in playing). Word order is adjective/noun modifiers before the head noun خوشنواز; پر here means “full (of)”, linking بیم (fear) to دل (heart).
segments.s647-g218: چنین تا is a temporal/consecutive frame 'thus until/so that'; the verb phrase بر کنده آمد فراز is a poetic intransitive cluster meaning 'sprang up / came up on high' with بر (on/upon) + کنده (lit. 'torn/uprooted', here idiomatically 'springing up') + آمد (past 'came') and فراز 'to a height/above'.
segments.s647-g219: برآمد is the past tense verb 'arose/issued forth' placed at the start for emphasis. ز introduces the source 'from', هردو modifies سپه ('both hosts/armies'), and the objects بوق و کوس are paired instrumental signals 'horn (trumpet) and drum.'
segments.s647-g220: هوا شد is an inchoative or copular construction 'the air became / the atmosphere was (affected)'. ز گرد سپاه indicates cause/source 'from the dust of the army', where گرد here means 'dust' and آبنوس qualifies سپاه as 'ebony/very dark (i.e., black-clad)'.
segments.s647-g221: چنان 'so/such' modifies تیرباران 'a rain/shower of arrows'; بد intensifies it as 'terrible/violent'. از هر دو روی is a prepositional phrase 'from each/both sides' with روی used as 'side/surface.'
segments.s647-g222: که introduces the result clause 'so that'; چون 'like/as' compares خون 'blood' to آب 'water'; اندر آمد 'came into/flowed into' uses اندر (in/within) + آمد (past 'came'), and به جوی 'into the stream' marks the destination.
segments.s647-g223: چو 'when' introduces a temporal clause; نزدیکی 'nearness' together with کنده شد (lit. 'was torn away') is an idiom meaning 'the distance was broken' — i.e., 'he came close'. خوشنواز is the subject 'the minstrel' mentioned after the verb cluster in poetic order.
segments.s647-g224: همی‌گفت is a past habitual form 'kept saying / would say'; با here functions as 'to/with' marking the addressee (داور 'the judge/arbiter'); پاک راز is the object 'the pure secret', with پاک qualifying راز.
segments.s647-g161: گفت is the past tense verb 'said' and is the main reporting verb; بدو is the enclitic prepositional pronoun 'to him' placed before the verb (classical order). نزدیک here functions as an imperative/command 'come near' (an archaic/poetic brief imperative), and پیروزِ رو is a vocative phrase 'O victorious-faced' (پیروز = 'victorious', رو = 'face', together an epithet addressing the addressee).
segments.s647-g162: به introduces the manner ('with' / adverbially) here; چربی is used poetically as a quality of speech ('smooth/unctuous/flattering') rather than literal 'fat' (no matching sense provided); سخن‌گوی describes the speaker ('eloquent'/'smooth-speaking'); و simply joins the clauses; پاسخ is 'answer'; شنو is an imperative 'hear!' (listen to the reply).
segments.s647-g163: بگویش is the imperative 'tell/say to him' (بگو + -ش 'to him'); که introduces a reported content clause 'that ...'; عهد نیای تو را is a definite object phrase ('the pledge/oath/pledge of your ancestor') with را marking the object — the following segment provides appositive description of that 'عهد'.
segments.s647-g164: This is an appositive noun phrase describing the previous object: بلند (adjective) precedes اختر ('lofty/loftiness' + 'star'), و links a second noun رهنمای ('guide, adviser'), and تو را again marks possession/definiteness — the whole reads 'your lofty star and your guide' as a characterization of the forefather's pledge.
segments.s647-g165: همی is an archaic/poetic particle that gives an emphatic/continuous sense (here marking the speaker's intention: 'I will/shall'); بر سر نیزه is a prepositional phrase 'on the head/tip of a spear' (بر + سر + نیزه); پیش سپاه means 'before/in front of the army/host' (پیش = 'before, in the presence of').
segments.s647-g166: بیارم is the first-person verb 'I will bring / I bring' in archaic/poetic form (here the main verb, though the lemma list doesn't show the verbal sense); چو introduces a simile 'like / as'; خورشید تابان به راه is the image compared ('the shining sun on its path'), with به راه marking location/manner — so 'I will bring it ... like the shining sun on the road.'
segments.s647-g167: بدان (here written as ب + دان) is a classical compound meaning 'by/with that' or 'thereupon' introducing purpose; تا introduces the purpose/result clause 'so that'; هر آنکس که دارد خرد is a relative clause 'whichever person has wisdom' (دارد present 'has, possesses' and خرد = 'wisdom').
segments.s647-g168: به introduces the object of viewing ('at/on'); منشور آن دادگر is a genitive construction 'the charter/inscribed tablet of that just ruler' (منشور = inscribed tablet/charter, آن دادگر = 'that just one' or 'that just ruler'); بنگرد is a third-person present-subjunctive/hortative 'may he look / let him look' (classical subjunctive form used after تا for purpose).
segments.s647-g113: بگویم is a 1st-person singular present/subjunctive form (literary) meaning 'let me say' or 'I say'; که introduces a reported clause ('that'); آن کرد here is a compact clause with آن ('that') + کرد (past form of 'to do') and places emphasis on the action or state being reported; بهرام گور follows as the subject/topic (proper name with epithet). Word order is literary: verb (بگویم) + complementiser (که) + demonstrative+verb (آن کرد) + name.
segments.s647-g114: به here functions as a prepositional marker introducing the qualities possessed ('with' / 'in'); a coordinated list follows: مردی (manliness), دانایی (wisdom), فر (royal glory, the Zoroastrian/epic 'farr'), زور (strength). The conjunction و simply links the nouns in a standard list of attributes.
segments.s647-g115: نمانم is 1st-person singular negative imperfective/literary form ('I did not remain' / 'I did not stay'); به جایی ('in/at any place') gives the location scope; پی before the noun is an adverb/preposition meaning 'after' or 'in pursuit of', and خوشنواز is a noun here ('the melodious one' / 'the minstrel') that the speaker follows. The clause literal order is negative-verb + locative phrase + directional/relational particle + object.
segments.s647-g116: به introduces destinations ('to'); هیتال and ترک are proper names (Hital likely a person/place; ترک = 'the Turk') joined by و; از here means 'from' indicating range or movement 'from نشیب و فراز' (low places and heights); نشیب و فراز is a pair meaning 'valleys/descents and peaks/ascents' (i.e. ups and downs). The whole segment specifies the routes/destinations the speaker followed without staying.
segments.s647-g117: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'; بشنید is the simple past 3rd-person form 'he heard'; فرزند خاقان ('the khagan's son') is the subject who performs the hearing; که begins the subordinate clause giving what was heard (that the king ...). The inversion (verb before subject) is common in epic/literary style.
segments.s647-g118: ز is the poetic form of از ('from' / 'across'); جیحون is the Amu Darya river; گذر کرد is a compound verbal idea 'passed/crossed' (گذر noun + کرد auxiliary 'did/made' giving past action); خود is emphatic 'himself' and با سپاه indicates accompaniment 'with (his) army'. Together: 'had crossed the Jeyhun himself with an army.'
segments.s647-g119: همی‌بشکند is a literary/imperfective form with همی- marking continuous or iterative action and بشکند (3rd-person) 'breaks' / 'was about to break' — here translated as 'was broken' or 'broke'; عهد بهرام گور is the noun phrase 'the pact/covenant of Bahram Gūr' (عهد governed by the name). The phrase presents the breaking of that specific treaty.
segments.s647-g120: بدان is a classical prepositional compound meaning 'by that / thereby' (here segmented as ب + دان; دان does not carry its usual 'know' sense and functions as part of the compound); تازه شد is 'became fresh' — idiomatically 'was renewed' or 'began anew'; the following nouns کشتن و جنگ و شور list the results: 'killing, war, and tumult/uproar'. The clause links the treaty's breaking to the renewal of violence.
segments.s647-g177: برین بر = بر این (literary order): a topical/adverbial phrase meaning 'to/for this' or 'concerning this'. جهاندار یزدان is a compound title 'the world‑ruler, God' (noun + epithet). گواست is an older/poetic contraction of گواَه است meaning 'is witness' (predicate comes last: [topic] + [subject-title] + [copula/predicate]).
segments.s647-g178: که introduces a content clause 'that...'. او را is the clitic object/topic marker on 'he' (here referring back to someone mentioned earlier). گوا (witness) + خواستن (to ask/request) forms an object‑infinitive: 'to ask him as a witness'. ناسزاست is a fixed predicative judgement 'it is unbecoming/insulting' (nasazast is a poetic evaluative formula).
segments.s647-g179: که here links the clause as an explanatory/subordinate clause ('for/that'). بیداد is an abstract noun 'injustice' and جوید is the present/poetic imperfect 'seeks' (جویدن). همی is an enclitic adverb 'always/continually' emphasizing ongoing action. جنگ من is a noun phrase 'war against me' or 'my war' (object/theme).
segments.s647-g180: چنین 'thus/so' modifies the manner of the action. با + verbal noun 构造 (سپه کردن) = 'by mustering/sending an army' (با + infinitival/gerundial idea). آهنگ من is 'my intent/attack' (آهنگ = aim/intention, here the target or direction of action), so the whole phrase means 'thus, by setting an army in motion against me'.
segments.s647-g181: نباشی is a negative present/future form meaning 'you will not be / you shall not be' (archaic/poetic negation of باشی). تو is the 2nd‑singular subject. زین جنگ here (literary order) = در این جنگ / 'in/with this war' (zīn used poetically). پیروزگر is a predicative/epithet meaning 'victor, triumphant one' describing the subject.
segments.s647-g182: نیابی is a negative-cum-modal 'you will not attain/find' (here 'you will not succeed'). مگر = 'unless / except'. ز (poetic form of از) = 'by/from'. اختر نیک = 'a favorable/auspicious star' (astrology: good fortune). بر at the end is a short locative/directional particle ('on/upon' or 'from above') completing the phrase 'by/on a fortunate star'.
segments.s647-g183: ازین پس = 'from now on / henceforth' (از + این + پس). نخواهم + infinitive (فرستاد) is the periphrastic future/volitional negative 'I will not send'. کس = 'anyone' (object of فرستاد). The word order is literary: temporal phrase first, then the verb phrase.
segments.s647-g184: بدین جنگ = (به + دین in the manuscript segmentation) 'for this war / in this war' (badly segmented here as ب + دین). یزدان مرا یار بس is a short prayer/statement: یزدان = 'God', مرا = 'to me' (object), یار = 'ally/companion', بس = 'enough/sufficient' (predicate). The order is emphatic: for this war, God is enough as my ally.
segments.s647-g121: دبیر جهاندیده is a noun phrase (دبیر 'the scribe/secretary' + epithet جهاندیده 'worldly‑wise'); را marks that phrase as the definite direct object/recipient of the action. خوشنواز is a nominal predicate here ('a skilled/melodious musician') placed before the verb—Persian permits an object + predicate sequence that the verb will complete.
segments.s647-g122: بفرمود is the honorific/polite past tense 'he ordered/commanded' (literary). تا introduces the purpose/result ('so that'), شد is the past of شدن 'became/was made', and بر او فراز is a prepositional/idiomatic locution meaning 'exalted/raised on high (over him)'; together they render a result clause: he ordered X so that (the person) became exalted.
segments.s647-g123: یکی before a noun functions 'someone/a certain (one)'. نامه is the direct object 'a letter'. بنوشت is an archaic/persianized past form of نوشتن 'wrote' (here meaning 'wrote/inscribed'); با آفرین is an adverbial phrase 'with praise' or 'with a formula of praise' that qualifies how the letter was written.
segments.s647-g124: ز is the poetic preposition 'from/of' (here introducing the source/content of the praise: 'praise of the Creator'). دادار is 'the Creator' (a divine epithet). بر شهریار زمین is a dative/recipient phrase in older Persian: بر = 'to/toward', شهریار 'sovereign/king', زمین 'of the earth/land' (specifying the king's domain).
segments.s647-g125: چنین is an adverb 'thus/so'. گفت here is the past 'he said' (the lexical sense 'he said' is intended though the provided senses list a different sense), and کز (literary contraction of که + ز) introduces a clause of reason/source 'that/from which'. عهد شاهان داد is a compact genitival phrase: عهد 'the covenant/oath', شاهان 'of kings', داد used as the abstract noun 'justice' — 'the justice of the kings' covenant' or 'by the kings' just covenant.'
segments.s647-g126: به گردی functions idiomatically: گرد/گردی 'dust' used as an insulting appellation ('dust, nothing'); نخوانمت is the negative present/future form 'I will not call/name you' (ن‑ + خوانَمت, with the object clitic ‑ت 'you'). خسرونژاد is an appositive/vocative 'O scion/descendant of Khosrow' addressing the addressee; the clause means 'I will not call you dust, O scion of Khosrow.'
segments.s647-g127: نه negates the clause; این بود is the demonstrative + past copula 'this was'. عهد نیاکان تو is a genitive noun phrase 'the covenant/oath of your ancestors' (تو as the possessor follows the noun in the usual Persian word order). The line declares that the thing just described is not what the ancestors pledged.
segments.s647-g128: گزیده is an adjective 'chosen/selected' qualifying جهاندار 'sovereign' (a lofty title); و links that to پاکان تو, where پاکان 'the pure/righteous (plural)' with the possessor تو following means 'your pure ones' or 'your righteous followers'. The phrase states what, the speaker says, was not the ancestral pact: making you chosen sovereign and your companions the pure.
segments.s647-g97: همی‌راند is a classical narrative-form verb meaning 'he rode on / was riding'; the prefix همی marks continuous or habitual action in epic style. با introduces accompaniment and links the following nouns as things he had with him; the list لشکر و گنج و ساز names 'army, treasure, and musical instruments' as co-objects of با.
segments.s647-g98: که begins a relative/purposive clause referring back to the group just mentioned ('who/that'); پیکار جویند is a present/habitual third-person plural (جویند = 'they seek/they are in search of'), here meaning 'they sought battle' or 'they went seeking battle'. با خوشنواز attaches as an instrumental/companional phrase: خوشنواز functions as a noun 'a skilful player/musician' accompanying their going to battle.
segments.s647-g99: نشانی is the head noun 'a mark/inscription'; که introduces a relative clause modifying نشانی. بهرام یل is a name plus title ('Bahram the hero'); کرده بود is the compound past (perfect) — کرده (past participle of کردن) + بود (auxiliary) — giving 'had made'.
segments.s647-g100: ز is the poetic preposition 'from'; پستی here means 'low-ness' or 'low place' (physical or figurative). بلندی would naturally mean 'height' but the provided lexeme senses don't list that sense here; برآورده بود is again past perfect (برآورده as verbal/adjectival compound + بود), meaning 'had lifted/raised up' or more generally 'had brought/put up'. The whole phrase states that he had raised it up from a low place to a height.
segments.s647-g101: نبشته is the noun 'inscription' or 'written text' placed first for topical emphasis. یکی here is 'a certain' (indefinite) qualifying عهد (a covenant/treaty); شاهنشهان is the genitive/attributive noun 'of the shahanshahs / emperors', so the phrase reads 'a certain covenant of the emperors.'
segments.s647-g102: که begins a clause explaining the content of the inscription: از here means 'concerning/of' (not purely spatial), followed by ترک و ایرانیان 'Turks and Iranians'; در جهان literally 'in the world' — together marking the inscription's scope or subject matter.
segments.s647-g103: کسی here functions with the negating elements to mean 'no one'; زین is the compound 'ز + این' meaning 'from this / beyond this' (the supplied lexeme senses do not include this deictic reading, so -1 is given). نشان is 'the marker/sign'. هیچ intensifies the negation ('at all/ever'); برنگذرد is a negative present/future-form verb meaning 'will not pass / shall not go beyond' (بر- + گذر + -د forms an epic negative).
segments.s647-g104: کزان is a compound of ز + آن meaning 'from which / from that place'; رود is the present-subjunctive/infinitival form of رفتن 'to go' here ('who goes' / 'whoever goes'), though the provided senses list noun senses for رود so we mark that mismatch with -1. برتر زمین is a noun phrase 'the foremost/superior of the land' and نشمرد is the negative past/present-future form of شماردن 'to count/reckon'—here 'will not count/shall not reckon'. Together: 'whoever goes from it will not be reckoned among the superior of the land.'
segments.s647-g17: خردمند is the subject (a wise person). هم نیز are additive/emphatic particles placed after the subject to mean “also/even” (literary order: subject + particles). جاوید نیست is the predicate: جاوید (eternal) + نیست (is not) yields “is not eternal / not immortal.”
segments.s647-g18: This clause is a comparative negative: [X] برتر از [Y] نیست = “X is not superior to Y.” فر جمشید is the standard of comparison (“the glory of Jamshid”). فری here is the compared term (an archaic/poetic noun for renown/glory); none of the supplied sense options exactly match that poetic sense, so its sense is marked -1. The verb نیست negates the whole comparative.
segments.s647-g19: چو is the temporal subordinating particle here meaning “when.” تاجش is the subject/possessive noun “his crown.” به ... اندر آمد is a poetic locution “came into / fell upon / shone into” (به + اندر آمد), with ماه (moon) as the locus; بمرد follows as the main past action (“he died” — a literary past form).
segments.s647-g20: نشست here functions as a noun/nominal notion of the royal seat/throne (a literary use of نشست = 'seat, sitting'); کیی (Kiani) qualifies it. دیگری را سپرد is the verb phrase: سپرد (entrusted/handed over) takes دیگری را as the recipient object with را marking the definite/particular recipient — overall: “he handed the Kayanian seat to another.”
segments.s647-g21: نماند + کس forms a negative-existential statement: literally “remained — person,” i.e. “no one remained.” برین is a poetic compound (بر + این) meaning “on this,” so برین خاک جاوید = “on this eternal earth” (خاک جاوید = ‘eternal earth’). Word order is literary but maps to “no one was left on this eternal earth.”
segments.s647-g22: ز هر بد = “from every evil” (ز = from/of, هر = every, بد = evil). به یزدان پناهید is an exhortation: پناهید is jussive/imperative plural “take refuge in / seek refuge with God (یزدان).” و بس is a closing formula “and that is enough” (i.e. nothing more is needed).
segments.s647-g23: همی‌بود is a literary imperfect/habitual or modal past (“there would be” / “it used to be / it would last”), followed by یک سال “one year.” با داد و پند links two nouns with با = “with”: داد (here = justice/fair rule) and پند (advice/counsel). Together: “There would be one year with justice and counsel.”
segments.s647-g24: This completes the idea: خردمند modifies the following phrase (the wise), وز is a poetic connective here meaning roughly “and/from” (the provided sense options don’t match this grammatical use, so marked -1). هر بدی بی‌گزند = “free from every harm” or “unharmed by every evil” (هر بدی = every evil, بی‌گزند = unharmed). The phrase says the wise would remain unharmed by any evil.
segments.s647-g225: وزان is an adverbial/composite preposition meaning 'from that' or 'from that side' and introduces direction; روی here means 'side' or 'surface' (so 'that side'). چون introduces a simile ('like/as') and is followed by باد ('wind'), producing the simile 'like the wind' which modifies the following name پیروزشاه (the subject). Word order is typical of poetic/archaic Persian: directional phrase + simile + name, with no copula.
segments.s647-g226: همی‌تاخت is an archaic/imperfective past tense form with the continuous particle همی‑, meaning 'charged'/'was charging'. The instrument/ accompaniment phrase با خوارمایه سپاه follows the verb: با introduces 'with' and خوارمایه qualifies سپاه (an insulting adjectival phrase 'lowly/worthless army').
segments.s647-g227: چو آمد is a temporal subordinate ('when he came'); چو here = 'when' and آمد is simple past 'came'. به نزدیکی is a locative phrase 'to/into the vicinity' (به + نزدیکی), and خوشنواز is the person (a proper name/epithet) placed after the locative, so the whole phrase means 'when Khushnavaz came near.'
segments.s647-g228: سپهدار ترکان is the subject phrase 'the commander of the Turks' (title + genitive). ازو is a contracted prepositional pronoun 'from him/away from him'. گشت is the past of گشتن meaning 'turned' (here 'turned back/away') and باز adds the sense 'back/again'—together ازو گشت باز = 'turned back from him.'
segments.s647-g229: عنان را بپیچید is a transitive past clause: عنان 'reins' is marked by the object marker را and the verb بپیچید (past stem پیچید with prefix ب‑) means 'turned' or 'twisted' (i.e. turned the horse). و links to بنمود پشت: بنمود is past of نمودن 'show/appear' and پشت 'back' (here 'his back'); Persian often omits the possessive pronoun, so 'بنمود پشت' = 'he showed his back' (i.e. turned away).
segments.s647-g230: پس = 'then', او here refers to the same person and stands before سپاه to indicate 'his army/host' in this archaic/poetic ordering. اندر آمد = 'came in/entered' (اندر = 'into/among', آمد = past 'came'). درشت qualifies the arrival ('large/forceful'), so the clause means 'then his great host came in behind him.'
segments.s647-g231: برانگیخت is a past verb meaning 'roused himself' or 'sprang up'. پس باره functions as an adverbial phrase 'then once more / on that occasion again' (باره here = 'occasion/instance'), and پیروزشاه is the subject (the name). The sequence places the temporal/iterative phrase between verb and name, typical of poetic word order.
segments.s647-g232: همی‌راند is the imperfective/continuous past 'was riding/driving on' (همی‑ continuous). با introduces instruments/accompaniment: گرز 'mace' and رومی کلاه 'Roman helmet' (رومی qualifies کلاه), so the phrase means he advanced/rode on with a mace and a Roman (Byzantine) helmet—either wielding the mace and wearing the helmet or having them as his equipment.
segments.s647-g185: This is a short clause: فرستاده (the messenger) is the agent, با نامه (with a letter) is an accompanying prepositional phrase, and آمد is the past tense verb 'came'. چو begins a temporal/subordinate clause ('when' / 'as') that connects to the next segment; گرد here is a poetic noun/adverb meaning 'together/around' or 'as a gathered mass' and prepares the image 'گردِ سخن‌ها' (a gathered cluster of words). Word order is V (آمد) after subject and its adjunct, and the conjunction چو attaches the following clause.
segments.s647-g186: سخن‌ها is the plural definite/indefinite 'the words' (object), به + پیروز marks the indirect object 'to the victor' (به = to/toward), بر یاد کرد is the verbal phrase: یاد کردن with the light verb کردن in the past (کرد), here 'to put into memory / to mention / to recall'. The verb comes at the end as is normal in Persian; the whole means 'he recalled / related the words to the victor.'
segments.s647-g187: چو is the temporal 'when'. برخواند is a past-tense compound verb (برد- / بر- + خواند) meaning 'read aloud' or 'read', taking آن نامه as its object ('that letter'). خوشنواز is the person named or epithet (the addressee/agent); in classical/poetic word order the subject can follow the verb and its object, so 'when Khushnavaaz read that letter'.
segments.s647-g188: پر از خشم شد is a predicative phrase meaning 'became full of anger' (پر = full of + از = 'of' here + خشم = anger; شد = became). شاه گردن‌فراز is the nominative phrase 'the proud (literally 'neck‑high') king' placed after the predicate for poetic inversion; together the clause reads 'the proud king became full of anger.'
segments.s647-g189: فرستاده را uses را as the marker for the person addressed/indirect object ('to the messenger'); گفت is the past verb 'said' and is followed by the content 'چندین سخن' ('several words'). In Persian the recipient with را can appear before the verb; the clause means 'he said several words to the messenger.'
segments.s647-g190: نگوید here is a negative imperative/subjunctive form 'do not say / do not tell' (polite or plural imperative), giving the content of what was said. جهاندیده مرد کهن is the addressee/target described: جهاندیده = 'worldly-wise / experienced', مرد = 'man', کهن = 'old/ancient', so 'O worldly‑wise, aged man' or more literally 'the worldly‑wise old man' — together the clause gives the command 'Do not tell the worldly‑wise old man.'
segments.s647-g191: که introduces a relative clause qualifying the preceding person ('who / that'). از چاچ 'from Chach' gives origin. یک پی نهد is an idiomatic sequence: پی here means 'a foot / a step' and نهد (نهاد) is the simple past/present-subjunctive form 'places / sets' — together 'sets/places one step' = 'advances a single step'. نزد رود means 'near the river' or 'toward the river'. The clause describes swift or small movement: 'who, from Chach, with one step reaches near the river.'
segments.s647-g192: به نوک سنانش is a prepositional phrase 'to the tip of his spear' (سنان + possessive ـش = his spear). فرستم is first‑person present/future 'I send' (poetic present often used for near future or emphatic statement). درود 'greeting' is the direct object: literally 'I send a greeting to the tip of his spear' — a poetic formula, here likely figurative/hostile, but grammatically straightforward: prepositional phrase + verb + object.
segments.s647-g265: The prepositional phrase به آهن ('with/by iron') precedes the verb; ببستند is the simple past 3rd-person plural 'they bound/tied'. پای قباد is the direct object (no modern را marker) and follows the verb in classical word order. The whole segment reads literally 'with iron they bound the foot of Qobad.'
segments.s647-g266: ز تخت و نژادش is a prepositional phrase 'of/from his throne and lineage' (نژادش = 'his lineage' with the enclitic pronoun -ش). یاد نکردند is negative past (نکردند) with یاد as the noun-object meaning 'remember/mention' — together 'they did not remember/make mention of'.
segments.s647-g267: چو introduces a temporal clause 'when'. آگاهی آمد is a simple past construction 'news/information came'. The word order places the destination phrase به ایران سپاه ('to/for Iran, the army') after the verb; read naturally as 'when the army in Iran received the news.'
segments.s647-g268: ازان = 'of/from that' (referring back to the previous fact). کنده is a participial form (from کندن) used adjectivally/passively here meaning 'torn/removed' (i.e. dethroned/removed). رزم پیروز is a compound attributive 'victorious in battle' placed before شاه ('king'), so the phrase apposes or describes the king mentioned in the news.
segments.s647-g269: خروشی برآمد is an idiomatic past phrase 'a cry/clamor arose'. ز کشور denotes the source 'from the country' or 'throughout the realm'. به درد is an adverbial complement 'in pain/sorrow' (difficulties/emotional grief). The verb برآمد governs the whole clause.
segments.s647-g270: ازان here means 'because of that' or 'from that' (pointing to the preceding event). شهریاران آزادمرد is a nominative/agent phrase 'the noble/lordly men' (شهریاران here 'lords' or leading men, آزادمرد 'noble/free men') — they are the source or cause of the cry.
segments.s647-g271: چو starts a temporal clause 'when'. اندر جهان این سخن گشت فاش is a classical word order meaning 'this speech/news became revealed in the world' — گشت is the past of 'become' and fاش 'open/revealed', so together 'became public.'
segments.s647-g272: فرود آمد is a compound verbal idea 'came down/descended' (فرود = descent, آمد = came). از تخت زرین بلاش is the source phrase 'from Balash's golden throne' — classical Persian often strings noun + adjective + name without an explicit izāfe, so تخت زرین بلاش = 'the golden throne of Balash.'
