segments.s647-g41: هر آنکس که ... is a relative clause introduced by 'هر آنکس که' = 'whoever/any person who'. دَارد is the present third-person singular 'has/possesses'. نهانی is an adjective/adverb 'hidden/secret' modifying غله (placed before the noun in this line for poetic word-order). The whole chunk reads literally 'whoever has hidden grain'.
segments.s647-g42: وگر ... وگر ... و گله lists alternatives using the archaic/poetic particle وگر (و اگر) meaning 'if/or if' (here functioning like 'or (whether) ... or ...'). The conjunction و joins the final item; گله is a noun 'flock/herd'. Word order is a simple enumerative listing of possessions.
segments.s647-g43: به نرخی فروشد is a phrase 'sells at a price' where به introduces the price/terms. فروشد here is narrative third-person (classical present/simple) 'he sells' (in modern Persian would be می‌فروشد). نرخی is used as 'a price' in context (a poetic noun form). او را هواست is an impersonal/stative construction: 'هوا داشتن' = 'to desire/feel inclined', and او را is the experiencer ('he is inclined'), with ست = 'is'.
segments.s647-g44: که introduces a subordinate clause explaining the wish. از here marks 'from/of' (source/partitive) before خوردنی جانور 'the eatable part of the animal' (خوردنی = edible). بی‌نواست is a predicative adjective 'is for the needy/poor' (بی‌نوا meaning 'needy/wretched') describing who should receive that food; the -ست ending is the copula.
segments.s647-g45: به here is the preposition 'to/for'. هر is the distributive determiner 'each/every' modifying کارداری; classical Persian often uses nouns like کارداری to mean 'steward/governor' (here a recipient). خودکامه‌ای is an adjectival/nominal form 'an autocrat' (the -ای ending gives indefiniteness/one of that sort). The whole phrase is an indirect-object phrase 'to every steward and autocrat'.
segments.s647-g46: فرستاد is simple past 'he sent/dispatched'. In classical word order the verb can come first, followed by indirect object (تازان 'to the raiders') and then the direct object (یکی نامه‌ای 'a certain/a single letter'); تازان is dative/plural 'to the raiders', یکی نامه‌ای = 'a letter' with the indefinite suffix -ی.
segments.s647-g47: که introduces a purpose/result clause 'that ...'. انبارها is the plural 'the storehouses'. برگشایند is an older/poetic third-person plural form (subjunctive/jussive/optative) meaning 'be opened' or 'they open' in the sense 'may they be opened' — here an imperative/optative sense linked to the preceding dispatch. باز is an adverb 'again' (or 'open' in context) and follows the verb for poetic word order.
segments.s647-g48: به گیتی = 'throughout the world' (به + گیتی). برآنکس literally 'upon that one' functions here as 'for that person' (i.e. the person in need). که هستش نیاز is a relative clause 'who is in need' where هستش is the copula 'is' (classical clitic form) and نیاز is the noun 'need'.
segments.s647-g81: This clause is a short V/O (object before verb) phrase typical of classical Persian: 'درم' (a dirham, money) is the object and 'داد' is the past tense verb 'gave/distributed'. The following prepositional phrase 'با لشکر نامدار' ('with a renowned army') attaches to the verb as an accompaniment/instrument. Together they state that he furnished funds and had a famed army at hand.
segments.s647-g82: 'سوی جنگ جستن' is an infinitival/purpose phrase meaning 'to go toward war'; 'سوی' = 'toward' + noun 'جنگ' = 'war' with the verbal noun 'جستن' = 'to seek/go'. 'برآراست کار' is a compound predicate: 'برآراست' (arranged/prepared) modifies 'کار' (the affair/task). Together the segment means he set about/prepared the affair of marching to war.
segments.s647-g83: Here 'بدان' is the fused prepositional idea ('to/at that' = به + آن) marking reference to the previously mentioned battle ('جنگ'). The next words name the leader: 'هرمز' is a personal name and 'بدی' is an epithet meaning 'evil' or 'wicked'; 'پیش‌رو' is an adjectival/nominal position 'foremost' (i.e. in front). Grammatically this says that Hormoz (styled 'the evil') was in the vanguard of that battle.
segments.s647-g84: 'همی‌رفت' is the imperfective/past progressive 'was going' (classical 'was proceeding'). The preposition 'با' introduces companions/instruments: 'کارسازان نو' = 'new, effective men' (literally 'those who make work succeed', i.e. effective/newly useful men). The whole clause modifies the vanguard action: he went forward accompanied by newly effective men.
segments.s647-g85: This segment names the subject 'قباد' (Qobad) and gives a locative/positional phrase 'از پس پشت' = 'from after/behind'. The phrase 'پیروز شاه' is an epithet-noun sequence 'the victorious king' and with 'از پس پشت' indicates Qobad's relative position (he comes from the rear of the victorious king). Word order places the name first, then the postpositional locative phrase.
segments.s647-g86: 'همی‌راند' is an imperfective past 'was driving' or 'drove' (he drove/moved forward). The simile 'چون باد' ('like the wind') modifies the manner of motion, and 'لشکر به راه' ('the army on/along the road') gives the object and direction. The prepositional 'به راه' here indicates motion 'along the road/toward the way'.
segments.s647-g87: This is a classical Persian possession-existence construction: 'X را Y بود' = 'X had Y' (literally 'there was Y to X'). Here 'که' introduces the clause ('for/that'), 'پیروز را' marks the possessor ('the victorious' with را), and 'پاک فرزند بود' states 'there was a pure son' — i.e. the victorious man had a virtuous/clean son. 'بود' is past tense 'was'.
segments.s647-g88: This nominal predicate gives the son's qualities: two adjectives 'خردمند' (wise) and 'شاخی برومند' (literally 'branchy/branch-like' + 'prosperous/noble') followed by 'بود' ('was'). In poetry 'شاخی' evokes a scion/offspring (a branch), so the phrase says the son was wise and a vigorous/noble scion. Adjectives precede the copula 'بود' in this classical word order.
segments.s647-g257: چنین آمد is an adverb + verb construction meaning 'thus it came/occurred' (the verb آمد precedes its subject here, a common poetic order). این چرخ ناپایدار is the subject phrase ('this unstable wheel'), where چرخ in epic poetry denotes the wheel of fortune/fate and ناپایدار qualifies it as fickle/unstable.
segments.s647-g258: The correlative construction چه ... و چه ... means 'whether ... or ...'. با marks the domain/target ('with/regarding'); زیردست = 'the subordinate/lowly' and شهریار = 'the king/sovereign'. The phrase says the wheel affects both low and high alike.
segments.s647-g259: بپیچاند is the 3rd‑person imperfective/epic present of پیچاند here meaning 'turn against / deceive / confound'. آن را is the definite object ('that one') and که introduces a relative clause: خود پرورد = 'which/whom it itself nurtured/raised' with پرورد used as a past/participial idea 'nurtured'.
segments.s647-g260: اگر ... شوی is a conditional clause 'if you become'. شوی is 2nd‑person singular present/subjunctive 'become/are' and پاسبان خرد is a compound noun phrase 'guardian/keeper of wisdom' where خرد is an abstract noun 'wisdom'.
segments.s647-g261: نماند is 'does not remain / will not be left' (ن- prefix expressing negation on مانَد). برین خاک = بر + ین (orthographic/poetic form of 'این') + خاک = 'on this soil/earth', modified by جاوید 'everlasting'. کس in the scope of the negation yields 'no one'.
segments.s647-g262: تو را is a dative/emphatic 'to you'; توشه 'provision' (metaphorical, e.g. for the journey after death) and از راستی 'made of / from truth/uprightness' (از marks source). باد here is the optative particle 'may it be', and و بس = 'and enough/that is all' closes the wish.
segments.s647-g263: چو = 'when' (temporal). بگذشت is a past verb 'passed by / went past'. The repeated بر marks 'over/against' or 'upon' and introduces two objects in parallel: کنده 'the uprooted' (literal or epithet) and خوشنواز which here functions as a name/epithet (literally 'melodious' / 'the one skilled in playing'). The line strings the two objects after the verb with repeated preposition.
segments.s647-g264: سپاهش is سپاه + possessive ش 'his army/host'; شد 'became'. از خواسته 'from (wanting)/desire' uses از to mark what is lacking, and بی‌نیاز is an adjective 'needless / self‑sufficient / free from want' describing the army's state.
segments.s647-g169: مرا آفرین بر تو نفرین بود: 'مرا' here is the fused object/dative pronoun ('me' / 'for me') formed from م + را (the را is the object/ dative marker). آفرین is a noun 'praise/acclamation' and بر + تو marks the target ('upon you'). نفرین بود is a copular clause with بود (past/old-style copula 'was') meaning 'was a curse'; overall the clause says that what was praise for the speaker turned into a curse for the addressee.
segments.s647-g170: همان نام تو شاه بی‌دین بود: همان is a demonstrative ('that' / 'that very'), followed by نام تو 'your name/reputation'; the clause is completed by the predicate 'شاه بی‌دین بود' ('was the impious king'), with بود again as the past copula. Word order is demonstrative + head noun + possessor + predicate.
segments.s647-g171: نه یزدان پسندد نه یزدان‌پرست: the repeated نه negates two parallel subjects. یزدان پسندد is Present/Tense-3sg (پسندد) 'God does not approve/accept', and یزدان‌پرست follows as a coordinated noun phrase 'nor the pious (worshippers of God)'. The verb پسندد governs the negation and the second noun is coordinated without repeating the verb.
segments.s647-g172: نه اندر جهان مردم زیردست: another negative clause introduced by نه; اندر جهان 'in the world' is a locative phrase, followed by مردم زیردست 'people/subjects under (his rule)'. زیردست is used as a noun/adjective 'subjects, subordinates'. The overall sense: there are no people in the world subject to him.
segments.s647-g173: که بیداد جوید کسی در جهان: که introduces a relative/conditional clause 'whoever/that who'; بیداد 'injustice/oppression' is the object of جوید (جویدن) here meaning 'seeks/asks for' and جوید is present/3sg. کسی is the indefinite 'someone/anyone' and در جهان marks 'in the world.' The clause thus describes 'anyone who seeks injustice in the world.'
segments.s647-g174: بپیچد سر از عهد شاهنشهان: the verb بپیچد (prefix بـ + پیچد) is a 3rd-person-form of پیچیدن 'to twist/turn' used here with the sense of being torn off/severed; سر is the direct object 'head'. از عهد 'from the era/line (عهد = age, epoch, here 'line/dynasty') and شاهنشهان 'of the emperors' form a genitival phrase. The clause is idiomatic: 'his head will be twisted/removed from the line of emperors' (i.e. he will be cut off from kingship).
segments.s647-g175: به داد و به مردی چو بهرام شاه: the preposition به introduces two adverbial qualities 'به داد' (in/for justice) and 'به مردی' (in/for manliness/valour). چو 'like/as' introduces the comparison 'like Bahram', and شاه appends the name as title. The phrase characterizes someone as equal in justice and manliness to Bahram the king.
segments.s647-g176: کسی نیز ننهاد بر سر کلاه: کسی نیز 'no one/even no one' (here کسی with the negative context means 'no one'), ننهاد is the negative past form (ن + نهاد) of نهادن 'to place/put', so 'placed not' = 'did not place'. بر سر کلاه is a noun phrase 'a hat upon the head.' Together: 'no one else put a hat on his head' (i.e. no one equalled him or assumed his station).
segments.s647-g137: چو is a temporal conjunction 'when'. آن نامه is the direct object 'that letter' (آن determiner + نامه). برخواند is a past/formal perfective verb 'read aloud' and here appears before its subject (typical in older Persian); پیروز شاه is the postposed subject ('the victorious king'), an epithet + noun.
segments.s647-g138: برآشفت is a past/punctiliar verb meaning 'became angry/was enraged'. زان is a fused prepositional/conjunctive form 'from that / because of that' linking the anger to what preceded. نامور qualifies پیشگاه — 'that famed/praised presence/court' — so the clause means he grew angry at/within that renowned presence.
segments.s647-g139: فرستاده را is the indirect/direct object marked by را ('to the messenger'). گفت is the reporting verb 'he said' introducing direct speech; برخیز و رو are imperatives inside that reported speech: برخیز 'stand up' and رو 'go / turn (toward)'. Note that گفت here introduces a command to the messenger.
segments.s647-g140: به نزدیک = 'toward near / to the vicinity of' (به + نزدیک). آن مرد دیوانه = 'that man, the madman' (آن determiner + مرد + adjectival noun دیوانه). شو is the 2nd/3rd person imperative of شدن 'become/go' used here as an intransitive imperative meaning 'go / be (there)' — 'become near' i.e. 'go near him'.
segments.s647-g141: بگویش is the imperative 'tell him' (بگو + ـیش). که introduces a subordinate clause 'that'. تا پیش رود here expresses the purpose or result 'so that he may go ahead' with a present/subjunctive verb رود (classical subjunctive). برک is the name/term of the person/things that will go ahead and is syntactically linked to the following clause that names the sender.
segments.s647-g142: شما را is the 2nd‑person object with the object marker را ('for you' / 'on your behalf'). فرستاد is the past verb 'sent'. بهرام چک is the agent placed after the verb (typical classical Persian V–O–S order): 'Bahram Chak sent (him/it) for you.' Taken together: 'Tell him that Bahram Chak has sent Barak to go ahead of you.'
segments.s647-g143: کنون = 'now'. تا here means 'as far as / up to' (marking an extent). لب رود جیحون = 'the bank/edge of the Jayhun (Amu Darya) river' (noun + governing noun). تو راست is a verbless predicate construction where راست ('right/true/held') predicates about تو 'you' — in context it indicates possession or rightful extent: 'up to the Jayhun's bank, it is yours.'
segments.s647-g144: بلندی و پستی و هامون lists landscape features: 'heights and low places and plains.' تو راست repeats the predicate 'they/it is yours' with تو the possessor and راست the adjectival/predicative word meaning 'right/held/your proper domain.' The series names the high and low ground and the plain that belong to (or fall under) the addressee.
segments.s647-g153: چو is a temporal subordinator meaning “when” introducing a time clause. گفتار (noun) is the object of بشنید: بشنید is a past, third-person singular perfective verb “he heard.” The clause contains two coordinated past actions joined by و, with نامه بخواند functioning in parallel: نامه is the direct object and بخواند is a past third‑person verb meaning “he read/recited.” Together the clause means “when he heard the speech and read the letter.”
segments.s647-g154: سپاه پراگنده is a noun + adjective phrase “the scattered army/host,” with پراگنده postmodifying سپاه. را marks the phrase as the definite direct object. برنشاند is a past, third‑person singular verb formed with the prefix بر‑ plus the past stem نشاند (to seat/place) and here means “he set/placed” or more idiomatically “he marshaled/mustered” the troops.
segments.s647-g155: بیاورد is a past third‑person singular of an older/perfective form meaning “he brought” (biā‑ + āvard). لشکر is the direct object (here not marked by را), and به + دشت نبرد is a prepositional phrase “to/onto the plain (دشت) of battle (نبرد),” where دشت نبرد is a compound/genitive-like construction “battle‑plain.” Word order (Verb + Object + PP) is typical of epic diction.
segments.s647-g156: همان qualifies عهد as “the same/that very” pledge/oath; را makes عهد the definite object. بر سر نیزه is a prepositional phrase literally “upon the head/top of the spear” (سر نیزه = spear‑head), and کرد is the past third‑person singular auxiliary/lexical verb “he did/made” used here to mean “he placed/caused” (i.e., he fixed that oath upon a spear).
segments.s647-g157: که introduces a relative/subordinate clause “that/which.” بستد is the past third‑person singular of بستن used with the sense “to bind/tie” (here idiomatically “to make a binding pledge” or “to swear”). نیایش is used as “prayer/supplication” and, with ز + proper name, ز بهرامشاه indicates the source/agent (‘by Bahram Shah’ or ‘on Bahram Shah’s behalf’). The clause therefore means roughly “that he bound (made) a vow/prayer from/by Bahram Shah.”
segments.s647-g158: که again introduces a clause: جیحون (the Amu Darya) is the subject and میانجیست is a present third‑person form meaning “serves as intermediary/mediates.” ما را is the clitic‑marked object “us” (را marks it as definite), and به راه is a prepositional phrase “to the road/way.” The literal relation is “that Jeyhun mediates (or stands between) us to the road,” i.e. “that Jeyhun serves as the boundary/mediator for us on the road.” Note the classical present‑tense ending ‑ست on میانجیست.
segments.s647-g159: یکی here is an indefinite determiner meaning “a certain” (not the numeral reading), followed by مرد “man.” بینادل is an archaic/uncertain epithet (textually rare; its exact meaning is debated) and functions as an adjective modifying مرد. و connects the second epithet چرب‌گوی, which is a compound adjective meaning “smooth‑tongued” or “flattering.” Together the phrase describes the selected man as “a certain man, بینادل and smooth‑tongued.”
segments.s647-g160: ز لشکر is a prepositional phrase “from the army.” گزین کردن is a light‑verb construction for “to choose/select”; here گزین (root) + کرد (past auxiliary) = “he selected.” با آبروی is an instrumental/qualifying phrase “with (a) reputation/with honour,” which can either modify the chosen man (“a man of reputation”) or describe the selection as done with regard to honour. The verb is past third singular.
segments.s647-g193: فرستاده آمد is a simple past clause: فرستاده (the messenger) + آمد (came). The prepositional phrase بر خوشنواز follows and uses بر in the sense 'toward/to' (classical/poetic usage) to mark the addressee, so the phrase means 'came to Khushnavaz.' Word order is Subject + Past Verb + prepositional phrase.
segments.s647-g194: فراوان سخن گفت is a verb phrase where فراوان (an adverb/adjective 'much/plentifully') modifies سخن گفتن ('to speak' — here سخن + گفت). گفت is the past (3rd-person) verb 'he spoke'. با او is the prepositional complement 'with him', and به راز is an adverbial phrase meaning 'in secret' (به + noun marking manner in classical Persian).
segments.s647-g195: که introduces reported content ('that' / 'who'). نزدیک پیروز is a descriptive phrase 'near (to) victorious' or 'close to victory' placed before the noun phrase ترس خدای ('fear of God'), where ترس خدای is a genitive/construct phrase ('fear of God') in inverted poetic order preparing the object of the following verb.
segments.s647-g196: ندیدم is first-person past 'I did not see'. نبودش is نبود + enclitic -ش ('to/for him'), here meaning 'there was not for him' = 'he had no...'. کسی رهنمای ('anyone [as] guide') is the noun phrase governed by نبودش; the whole clause therefore conveys 'I did not see that he had any guide' or more simply 'he had no guide'. Note the poetic ordering where the negated existential idea is expressed by نبودش + noun phrase.
segments.s647-g197: همه is 'all/everyone' (here indicating 'all people' or 'everyone'); دیدمش is a past-tense reporting verb 'saw him' (poetic register allows compact forms); جنگ جوید همی is a present/habitual expression: جوید (classical present stem 'seeks/desires') with همی giving continuous aspect — 'he continually sought war.' The clause juxtaposes observers' view and the man's habitual behavior.
segments.s647-g198: به فرمان یزدان is an instrumental/authorial prepositional phrase 'by the command of God' (به marking the cause/authority in classical usage). نگوید همی is a present/gnomic-form verb: ن‑ as the classical negative prefix + گوید (he says) yields 'he does not say', and همی again adds habitual/continuous aspect — 'he continually does not say [that it is by God's command]'. The clause therefore denies that he attributes his acts to divine command (literal: 'does not say [so]').
segments.s647-g199: چو is the temporal conjunction 'when'. بشندی here is a classical/poetic past form meaning 'he heard' (used with چو to mean 'when he heard'); ز (poetic form of از) + او = 'from him', so زو این سخن = 'this speech from him'. خوشنواز is the addressee/name. Together: 'When Khushnavaz heard this speech from him...'.
segments.s647-g200: به یزدان پناهید is a past/clause meaning 'he sought refuge in God' (پناهید = take refuge). و links the two actions. بردش نماز is a compact poetic expression: برد (past of بردن, 'took/carried/led') + ـش (object 'him') + نماز ('prayer'), idiomatically rendered 'he performed/said prayers for him' or 'he took him to prayer.' The clause therefore records two consecutive actions: seeking God's refuge and offering prayer on his behalf.
